speaker: interviewer
text: You were taken from the ghetto to a prison, where you had good treatment--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. The prison was in the ghetto, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from the prison, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gather up quite a few hundred people, there. Like, they took me out on the morning, you know, middle of the night, from my apartment, they took out other people. And they gathered us in that prison. And obviously, when they had enough for transport, what the Germans demanded-- a few hundred, I don't know how many, but it was in the hundreds. And they send us away, took us on a train one morning. They gave us a whole loaf of bread, I remember. And by the way, we finished it on the train. We didn't wait too long, because that was so tempting, the hunger, that we finished. They told us we could travel a few days, they don't know. They didn't know probably themselves, in the ghetto, where we were going, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a train was it? Was it a normal train, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, it wasn't a passenger train. It was freight, a freight train. We were closed in there. And in fact, the seat that I was using-- it was very important in the ghetto and in the camps, wherever you went, to have a pot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on a belt, I had a pot and a cup and a spoon. That was, that was-- you wear, like, weapons. So I took off that pot, turned it upside-down, and sat down. This was my seat. Instead of sitting on the floor, that was-- There were no seats in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, freight, freight, just freight trains. What you see, however, with doors closed, locked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say it was overcrowded. They had put, I don't know, 70 or 80 people in. No, it wasn't particularly-- not this train, really, was crowded. They had enough trains to take us. And we wound up in [? Tschenstochova-- ?] in German, Tschenstochau. And we walked on foot to a factory, which we later found out was an ammunition factory. It produced mostly bullets for rifles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I had no trade, you know, being a mechanic or a machinist, or whatever. I was, in the beginning, sweeping the floors from the scrapings, whatever got dirty. In the machines, let's say, cut the bullets to a certain size. When the bullets were not filled with powder yet-- I don't know, I forgot what [? it was. ?] [NON-ENGLISH]-- in German, it's [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] is the one that you put the powder in. The [NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE]. But the machines were automatic, and they cut off and used the fall-off material, and used to sweep that together, put it together. They took it away, melted it and became again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In barracks. We know that these were horse stables before the war. Horse stables, and they converted them into barracks to live. They put up barracks-- it was like one bed. But they had the straw inserted in material. And everybody had one of these. But it was one through the whole barrack. Quite a few hundred people in the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what clothing did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I brought. This was the lucky thing, that I brought a backpack from the ghetto with clothes. We were allowed to take clothing. That, that, this clothing-- not all of it, part of it-- I put on. We didn't know for sure whether they'll make us part with the backpack or not. As a precautionary measure, I remember, I put on three pairs of pants, three shirts, other things. In fact, the fact was, they took away the backpacks from us. And lucky for me, I was left, that I had to spare, slacks that I could sell later and feed myself well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for, for-- from March, from March, 1944, to January, 1945, I was eating pretty decent. In this munitions factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the munition factory, yeah. I substituted with my food that I bought from the Polish people who were brought in, by selling, like I said, a shirt, socks, slacks. That was a lucky thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the Polish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in from the outside every day to the factory. We came in from the camp. In other words, the camp had an entrance there. They counted every day, how many. And Poles came from the outside, from another end, where we couldn't get to. When they went home, to their homes where they were living, they had to go through the guards. They were dressed civilian clothes, proper. We wore civilian clothes. However, our clothes were marked with paint on the back-- white paint, a stripe of white paint, so we could be recognized. In other words, we couldn't go through the gate, there. They would recognize us, anyway. Besides, they had the documents to enter and to exit. So they exited one way. We exited-- let's say, let's say, if they exit out to the streets from the east, we have to go from the west to our camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any interaction between the Jews and the Poles? Or what was their--

speaker: interviewee
text: Say it, say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: --attitude? What was the attitude of the other Polish workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew what we were going through. And usually, people pity. They were not as anti-Semitic as before the war, you saw, when some Jews were prosperous. Here, they had all poor people. And they knew they were killing Jews, so there was a little sympathy there for us. So it was not, in the factory, where we met, it wasn't like it was before the war-- get the Jew. No, not that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there for a year--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Not a, not a-- No, no. It was from March, 1944, to Jan-- I would say 9, 10 months. January, yes, we left--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what happened-- the Russian Army started approaching in Poland from the Eastern Front. And obviously, they came to certain places where the Germans wanted to evacuate. In fact, first, they came, the Russian Army, to [NON-ENGLISH], which was also a munition factory. And before that, they evacuated them to us, because we were further inland west, closer to the west. As the Russians approached, they evacuated our camp. And again, they took us, one morning, to the trains-- again, freight trains. And we wound up in Germany, in Buchenwald. So two days or three days of traveling-- I don't remember exactly how long it took, and we were in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did Buchenwald-- when you arrived by train, what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I see? The train was a ramp that we had to walk a while to get to the camp. And I remember distinctly one thing-- when we came before the camp, and I saw this iron gate, and I saw, arbeit macht frei-- labor makes you free. And there was a big clock, there. I don't remember what time it was. But I said to myself, I'm walking in here, I don't know if I'll ever walk out from here. I'll never see this face of this clock again. That's what I said to myself. That's what I thought. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got off the train, who was in charge, and what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had the German guards that they took us to the camp, walked out with-- They were ready. When we came with the train, there were ready. They guarded the rail track where we arrived from both sides and from all over. And they took us walking into the camp. So these people-- once we came to the camp, they handed us over. They counted-- you know, it accounts for-- These guys left and the guards from the camp took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we had to disrobe. Now, this is a new chapter in itself. They took us to a bath. So, we had to disrobe to the bird suit-- naked, completely naked. Had to drop everything, leave it in a pile-- the suit-- went into a bath. Went, I remember, it was a big tub, a very big tub-- 50, 60 people could go in one shot. And it was filled with chemicals, and there was anti-lice, to kill lice. And before we went in there, a barber cut, shaved-- not shaved, but he took off, with a machine, all the hair, from all over-- under arms, armpits, all over, wherever a person has hair, they cleaned it off. And where we went into that [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call it, to kill the lice, that was burning, that chemical was burning like hell. Obviously, it went with the machine too close under the armpits and ripped some skin off. Didn't feel it before, but you went in-- and my God, [INAUDIBLE], burning like hell. But maybe this was a must because they tried to eliminate lice, because the sanitary conditions were bad-- no hot water, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that, after--

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we were assigned. They took us to barracks. And the barracks were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get your own clothes back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. [LAUGHS] Went out the other side. You know, in fact, it was January of 1944. I remember, now when we had to go from the bathhouse to get to a place where they assign you clothing-- they give you slacks, or whatever, a top-- we had to go through snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Towels to wipe you off? No, forget about towels. Nature dried you up. I had to go in January weather, which was cold, to go brand naked and wet, through the outside, outdoors, where we came into the [NON-ENGLISH], to the room where they assigned the clothes. That was another thing. How we survived, didn't get pneumonia, I cannot say. I don't know. Was it a prison uniform that they gave you or regular street clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at that time, it was street clothes. Obviously, they ran out. Too many people came from different directions to Buchenwald, and they ran out of the striped clothes. They ran out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they, we-- I got, happened to get good, good clothes, but it was marked. We had to mark with a yellow sign, and a number was put onto every person. I don't remember-- I remember it was in the 112,000. But I don't remember the exact number, so I don't want to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was that. Entirely different clothing than yours. In other words, whatever possessions you had from previously you possessed, everything was gone-- period. Lucky that I have relatives here in the United States, that I have a picture of my father and mother. I have a few more pictures of New York. But I didn't know it, so I didn't take them along, I'm sorry. But at least my relatives here gave me, and I have a few pictures from my parents, or from the siblings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which we'll see later. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have, here, one that I'll show you later, and you'll have it for the record.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you left the place where they gave you the clothing, where did you go then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were assigned to barracks. Again, it was like in [? Tschenstochova, ?] there, in the munition camp-- big barrack, but this wasn't a stable, because it was set up barracks for prisoners. But the bed, like I say, was four-high. It was one bed. There was another one, so when you came into the barrack, this was full of beds on the right-hand side and full on the left-hand side. The middle was a stove, there, you know, heating, with a pipe going out. But the beds were on the right and the left-hand side. And it was the single bed. It was a four-high, made from wood. You could sleep one next to the other. Here, we didn't have no straw, no nothing. It was just boards clipped together. And here, already, we slept like sardines. Here, it was crowded. Because obviously, they brought too many people in at the same time they had to evacuate, and it was very crowded. Like, you sleep on one side, and you wanted to turn at night, quite a few-- maybe a dozen people-- had to turn, wake up, turn the other side [INAUDIBLE]. That's how-- sardines, pressed as sardines in a can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any job in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in this camp. I remember, I was there about two weeks, and then they sent us to another camp-- from Buchenwald to Dora. There, already-- again, we went through the same thing-- all the clothing were dropped. A bath-- the same procedure. Cutting the hair-- whatever you had, you had to go through that, the same procedure as you arrived in Buchenwald. Again, different clothing-- there, I got very bad clothing, and it was winter, I want you to know, and it was cold. And there, I got already, the striped clothes. They had more available. And it was so thin. When you went out-- not only it was cold, but the wind nearly went through. It was very thin weaving. It wasn't a good, thick material. It was thin. And it was cold, and we worked outside. It just so happened, like I said before, I wasn't a machinist. There, in this particular camp, they made the V1, V2 rockets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camp Dora?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dora, yes. And that factory was hollowed out in a mountain. This could never be bombed. The Russians tried to bomb it. It didn't do a thing. It didn't even shake. It was a big mountain, and it was hollowed out, a big factory. When you walked in there, in that factory, you wouldn't believe that this is in a mountain. That's how it was done. Could never, never could happen anything to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went in there, what did you do? What work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, I wasn't a machinist. I was assigned work outside. So it was improvised work, like, like, it wasn't necessary. We had to carry rocks from one place to the next. And when it was all finished, we had to carry it back, to keep us occupied and, and, how do you say, like torture. OK, that was my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, I wasn't there too long-- maybe six, seven days-- another transport, they took us away. I believe, I believe it was the same mountain-- but around we went, from another side, to build, starting to build another factory like this. So there, it was real work-- had to mine it. We had German engineers drilling holes, putting in dynamite, ripping out, and when a rock got loose, we lay tracks, and we had to pick it up by hand, fill up the tracks, take it out, and so they can go further.

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, we started building another factory like the one existed in Dora.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is in your-- you had no coat or anything when you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had what I said, this clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not warmer clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not warmer clothing, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the food, and how about the beds?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, we had to get up in the morning, every morning, to go to this work. I got so used to this work, because I had one of the people who took care of the barrack-- he was a German from Vienna, happened to be a very young man. I don't know why he picked me, but he beated me every day for no reason at all. He started beating me with a, with a rubber-- it was covered with rubber, something. In fact, after the war, when I complained to the Germans, I said, this [NON-ENGLISH], this guy hit me over the head constantly. And they took me for x-rays. I complained on that. I told them what happened in that camp. He just picked on me, I don't know why. It was, it came to a point where I told the kapo-- it was a Polish guy, what he worked-- I was young, work didn't mean a thing. Even though I didn't have enough food, I worked. Because I didn't want to get beatings for saying, hey, you're standing around. So I worked and I didn't get beaten. When we came to march home, I told this-- it was a Polish kapo, and he was a little sympathetic, this guy. And I said, [? Ludzig, ?] [? Ludzig-- ?] well, I remember his name, so many years-- [? Ludzig, ?] I don't want to go back to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, why? You're finished work, the other shift has got to come in. You've got to go. I said, the [NON-ENGLISH], he's beating the hell out of me every day for no reason. I don't know why he's beating me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd rather-- the other shift, I'll go in a corner, I'll lay down to sleep, there, you know, in the mine where we took-- it wasn't a mine, because it was ground-level. But it was like a mine, the same work, obviously, like a mine, to hollow out. He said, this can't be done. They're counting how many would go in. We got to go out the same amount. You can't, can't do it. I'll talk to him. Well, I had to go back to the barracks. But it didn't help much. He spoke to him. It didn't help much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that camp, one day he gave me a beating with the fist. And he knocked off a piece of bone, and then he hit me in my teeth. My teeth didn't break. He knocked off a piece of bone from his fist, here, one of these bones.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how do you say, it came-- puss. He start--

speaker: interviewer
text: It got infected.

speaker: interviewee
text: It got infected. I don't know from what. Then he started beating me again, that I got poison in my teeth. I said, hey, what am I doing to you? Why are you doing that to me? He just kept hitting. But he had already his right arm in a sling, and the left wasn't so good, so it wasn't so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is a story you must listen. One day, he comes in-- this guy-- comes in and says, hold it. I've got an announcement for everybody in the barrack, there. OK, quiet. You could hear a fly, that's how quiet it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, everybody give the gold out that you have. We know you Jews have gold. Give the gold out, or we'll kill you. If we search you, we find gold, we'll kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We said, hey, in Buchenwald, they took our clothing away, they gave us other clothes. In Dora, they took the Buchenwald clothes away, they gave us other clothes. Where do we have gold from? No, you Jews have gold, you better surrender it. You know what? People who got clothes-- one guy found in his buttons, covered with the material, gold. And he went over to the [? oberkapo, ?] from the, the head of this. And he told him, listen, I got some gold. Didn't tell them he found it-- I got some gold, and he gave him-- can you give me a piece of bread? And the kapo said, sure. But he saw, if one Jew has gold, so the other Jews have gold, not realizing that the guy didn't want to say he found it in his uniform that he got assigned.

speaker: interviewee
text: With God's help-- even though I'm not religious, I'm still saying that-- this guy that gave the kapo, they started beating him so hard for more gold. And he had given already everything what he had for the bread, for the food that he received, the extra food. And he didn't have no more. They gave him such a beating that-- you know, every day we used to go out from the barracks. They used to call it an [NON-ENGLISH]-- an appeal. And we used to stand like soldiers-- 5 in a row, 100 in the length. And they used to count-- 5-4-- that anybody is missing. Now, like the SS men went through with the stick, counting. When it came to him, he said to the SS guard-- it was a higher rank-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- I want to tell you something that's very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the guy just took his number, and after he counted everybody, the count was fine. He went back to the barracks-- prisoner number blah, blah, blah, please come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took him on the SS. Obviously, he told him, [NON-ENGLISH], I found in my uniform that got assigned gold, and I wanted some extra money. I gave it to him. He went to the kapo they had, and they gave me a little extra food. But I haven't got no more. I gave him all I had. And now they're beating me for no reason. The SS man said nothing-- OK, got your story. Go back to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, that head kapo takes him into his little office, there in our barrack, because he was separated-- didn't sleep with us, had cut off for himself, like, a separate room. What happened there? He gave him a beating, and he told him

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told him, I had to tell him that this guy that gave him the gold, and he gave it to you. As soon as he went out, they took him in the washroom where we washed every morning, cold water, put a two-by-four over his neck. They stepped on it, and they killed him. So now, he can't blame it on them. However, this young man that hit me, that guy from Vienna-- he was maybe 19, 20-- he must have committed a crime, because no Germans or Viennese, Austrians, were in camp for no reason. He must have had murder, or whatever. I don't know what he was. They were afraid-- the kapo with his gang, big shots-- were afraid he is going to sing to the SS. They sent them out-- no more [NON-ENGLISH], no more in the barracks. They send him out on a commander to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while we work downstairs, digging out, dynamiting for a new factory, they had, on top of the mountain, drills that they drilled to get in air. In-- how do you call the soil-- see, I'm short on words. How do you call the soil that water doesn't go through? And they have that sometimes for cemeteries? No drainage?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's certain soil that no water goes through. I forgot what you call it. It's yellowish-reddish. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we had to climb up that mountain. And that was that soil. And whenever it was wet, you used to go in, and we had the-- from wood, shoes-- wood, not leather. They took this guy, this [NON-ENGLISH] from Vienna, this guy, and they made him go up there. And while going up there, went into this-- his shoe got stuck. He wanted to go back. They said, he couldn't go back. Anyway, I see, we have to stop now for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but was that quicksand? Are you talking about quicksand?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no quicksand. No, I'll, I'll probably remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clay, OK, now, I-- I say words, I'm missing clay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clay. And did he ever come out of there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed him. They brought him home dead, but everybody had to be accounted for. So that was the end of my beatings in this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. We'll go to the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.
